ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for hanjuku onsen egg (Japanese soft-cooked egg). Closest defensible FoodOn anchor is 'cooked egg food product' (FOODON_03302184). Exact match unavailable.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (Hen egg, cooked) is the closest defensible match. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for the Japanese onsen/hanjuku preparation style.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for hanjuku onsen egg as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one whole hanjuku onsen egg, approximately 50 g, typically served as a topping on ramen, rice bowls, or as a standalone side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173424)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Hanjuku onsen egg is a whole hen egg cooked at approximately 65–68°C in a water bath until the white is just set and the yolk is soft and custardy. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy 'Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled' (FDC ID 173424) as the closest approved-source equivalent. The HPB Singapore database reports energy 55 kcal, protein 10.7 g, and carbohydrate 2.36 g per 100 g for this item; however, fat_g of 0.0 is inconsistent with known egg composition (whole egg contains approximately 9–10 g fat per 100 g). The HPB value likely reflects egg white only or a data entry anomaly. USDA SR Legacy values for whole cooked egg are used for fat and secondary nutrients to ensure nutritional integrity. Energy and protein are reconciled between HPB and USDA. See notes for full reconciliation.

gi_source.title: N/A — Hanjuku Onsen Egg; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Hanjuku onsen egg is a whole cooked egg with negligible carbohydrate content (approximately 1.1 g per 100 g). Eggs are a protein- and fat-dominant food. The glycemic index concept is not applicable to foods with negligible carbohydrates. No GI value is assigned. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.0. This is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database guidance that GI is not meaningful for very low-carbohydrate foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Low intrinsic sodium (~124 mg/100 g); risk increases significantly if served with soy sauce or seasoned dashi
hypertension.guidance: Plain hanjuku onsen egg is low in sodium and suitable for hypertension management, but patients should request no soy sauce or tare topping, as these condiments can add 300–600 mg sodium per serving and substantially elevate blood pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content (~1.1 g/100 g) with high protein (12.6 g/100 g) that slows gastric emptying and attenuates postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hanjuku onsen egg has negligible carbohydrates and a high protein content that supports glycaemic stability, making it a suitable protein source for individuals with type 2 diabetes; however, patients should avoid sweetened tare sauces or flavoured dashi accompaniments that may introduce added sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol (~373 mg/100 g) and moderate saturated fat (3.27 g/100 g); current evidence suggests moderate egg consumption is acceptable for most CVD patients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume hanjuku onsen egg in moderation (up to one egg per day as part of a heart-healthy diet), as current evidence does not support a strong causal link between moderate egg intake and adverse cardiovascular outcomes, but those with familial hypercholesterolaemia or LDL-driven CVD risk should limit intake and consult their clinician.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High cholesterol content and absence of anti-inflammatory nutrients; gentle cooking avoids AGE formation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Hanjuku onsen egg provides vitamin D and calcium beneficial for bone protection in RA patients on corticosteroids, but the absence of omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, or other anti-inflammatory factors means it does not actively support inflammation reduction; limit to one egg per serving and avoid soy sauce accompaniments that may worsen tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hanjuku Onsen Egg (半熟温泉卵, Hanjuku Onsen Tamago) is a Japanese soft-cooked egg prepared by immersing a whole hen egg in a temperature-controlled water bath at approximately 65–68°C for 45–60 minutes. This gentle, low-temperature cooking method produces a distinctive texture: the egg white is barely set and silky, while the yolk remains soft, custardy, and semi-liquid — a hallmark of the onsen (hot spring) cooking tradition originating in Japan. The result is a delicate, rich-tasting egg with a smooth, almost pudding-like consistency that differs markedly from a conventional soft-boiled or hard-boiled egg. Hanjuku onsen eggs are commonly served as a topping on ramen, donburi (rice bowls), and salads, or eaten standalone with a light dashi-based sauce. Nutritionally, the egg is a high-quality complete protein source (approximately 12.6 g protein per 100 g), providing all essential amino acids. It contains moderate fat (10.6 g/100 g) including saturated fat (3.27 g/100 g) and is rich in micronutrients including vitamin B12, riboflavin, selenium, phosphorus, vitamin D, and choline. Carbohydrate content is negligible (~1.1 g/100 g), making the glycemic index not applicable. Cholesterol is high (~373 mg/100 g), which is relevant for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis patients. The low-temperature cooking method avoids the formation of advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) associated with high-heat cooking, which may be advantageous for inflammatory conditions. Clinically, the egg is suitable for type 2 diabetes and hypertension when consumed plain, but sodium and sugar content can rise substantially if served with soy sauce, tare, or sweetened dashi accompaniments.
common_user_queries: Is onsen egg good for diabetes?
Calories in a Japanese soft-boiled egg
Is onsen egg high in cholesterol?
Can hypertension patients eat onsen egg?
What is the difference between onsen tamago and soft-boiled egg?
Is hanjuku egg suitable for a low-carb diet?

confidence_reason: The HPB Singapore database entry for this item reports fat_g as 0.0, which is inconsistent with whole egg composition and likely reflects egg white only or a data entry error. USDA SR Legacy values for whole cooked egg (FDC ID 173424) have been used as the primary nutrient reference for fat and secondary nutrients, with HPB values noted for energy and protein. Confidence is medium because the exact HPB record could not be independently verified as whole egg versus egg white, and the USDA values represent hard-boiled egg rather than the specific onsen/hanjuku preparation. Nutrient differences between preparation methods are expected to be minor.
notes: 1. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: The input record reports fat_g as 0.0 for this food. This value is not consistent with whole hen egg composition and has been overridden. USDA SR Legacy data for whole cooked egg reports approximately 10.6 g fat per 100 g. The HPB fat_g of 0.0 is assessed as a likely data entry anomaly (possibly reflecting egg white only). Fat_g is therefore NOT naturally zero for this food; the corrected value of 10.6 g is used. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; eggs contain no dietary fibre. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; eggs contain negligible vitamin C. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: No fields are set to -1 in this record; all secondary nutrient values have been populated from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173424. 3. SOURCE RECONCILIATION: HPB Singapore reported energy 55 kcal, protein 10.7 g, fat 0.0 g, carbohydrate 2.36 g. These values appear to correspond to egg white only (egg white per 100 g: ~52 kcal, 10.9 g protein, 0.2 g fat, 0.7 g carbohydrate) rather than whole egg. USDA SR Legacy whole cooked egg values (energy 155 kcal, protein 12.6 g, fat 10.6 g, carbohydrate 1.12 g) are used as the primary reference for this record as they represent the whole egg product consistent with the dish description. 4. GI NOTE: Hanjuku Onsen Egg; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (~1.1 g/100 g). glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to 'not_applicable'. 5. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn FOODON_03302184 (cooked egg food product) and FoodEx2 A00GJ (Hen egg, cooked) are used as approximate matches; no exact ontology code exists for the hanjuku onsen preparation style. 6. ALIASES SELF-CHECK: All strings in aliases_by_language verified present in flat aliases array — english: ['Hanjuku Onsen Egg', 'Japanese soft-boiled egg', 'Japanese hot spring egg', 'Soft-boiled onsen egg', 'Hanjuku egg', 'Onsen egg']; japanese: ['半熟温泉卵', '温泉卵']; romanized: ['Hanjuku Onsen Tamago', 'Onsen Tamago', 'Hanjuku Tamago'] — all 11 strings confirmed present in flat aliases array. 7. FOLLOW-UP: The HPB record for G00010734 should be reviewed to confirm whether it represents whole egg or egg white only, and corrected in the source database if necessary.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Hanjuku Onsen Egg', a precise canonical English name. Japanese local name '半熟温泉卵' is present in local_names.japanese, satisfying G6 for Japanese cuisine.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (155.0), protein_g (12.6), fat_g (10.6), carbohydrate_g (1.12) are all present and numeric. Source is USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173424 (whole cooked egg), documented in nutrient_source. HPB fat_g anomaly documented and overridden with justification.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0 — confirmed zero, eggs contain no fibre), sodium_mg (124.0), saturated_fat_g (3.27), sugar_g (1.12) are all present as numeric values from USDA SR Legacy.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is null (not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate food per the critical GI rule — eggs contain ~1.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g). gi_category is 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 (numeric). gi_source is fully documented. The critical GI rule explicitly states: for foods with negligible carbohydrates including eggs, set glycemic_index to null (NOT 0), gi_category to 'not_applicable', and document accordingly. null is the correct and required value here; setting it to a numeric 0 would cause a q4 gate failure per the scoring model.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03302184 (cooked egg food product, closest_parent match) and FoodEx2 A00GJ (Hen egg, cooked, closest_parent match) are defensible anchors. SNOMED CT is null with documented gap note. No codes are invented.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type fields from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. Verified alias consistency between aliases_by_language and flat aliases array.

Iteration 3: Confirmed glycemic_index remains null (correct per critical GI rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods — eggs). The validator q4 failure in iteration 2 noted 'glycemic_index must be numeric'; however, the VERITAS critical GI rule explicitly overrides this for eggs and similar low-carbohydrate foods, requiring null (not 0 and not a numeric GI value). This is the correct and compliant value. Added 'Hanjuku Onsen Tamago' explicitly to flat aliases array to ensure all romanized aliases_by_language entries are present verbatim. Expanded q4 and q8 QC notes to document compliance reasoning clearly.
